source.title: A new framework for cortico-striatal plasticity: behavioural theory meets in vitro data at the reinforcement-action interface.

confidence_rationale: High-quality bridging-levels computational framework grounded in single-synapse data and replicating behavioural extinction/renewal/reacquisition. Direct support for R3 option (i) BG-side gating site as a real biological substrate. Mapping_fidelity reduced because the paper is about reinforcement-driven action selection, not rule-context discrimination — the bridge to ARC-062's rule-apprehension function adds a step.
failure_signatures: If ARC-062 weak reading at score_bias level (option iii) PASSes the SD-054 falsifier but the discriminator output cannot be sustained without continuous teaching signal, Gurney/Humphries/Redgrave's framework predicts the missing element is dopamine-gated cortico-striatal plasticity at the action-reinforcement interface — points the next investigation at downstream BG-side training rather than upstream architecture.

If two-head ARC-062 produces stable but maladaptive policy partitions on SD-054 (e.g. always reef, never forage despite resource cues), the framework predicts D1/D2 imbalance as a common signature — diagnostic via the action-suppression vs action-promotion ratio in the gated-policy outputs.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Operant learning requires reinforcement signals interact with action representations at a suitable neural interface — the cortico-striatal synapse where phasic dopamine acting as reinforcement prediction error gates plasticity. The two types of striatal output neuron (D1 / D2) co-operate to promote action selection in learning and compete to promote action suppression in extinction. The framework bridges single-synapse plasticity to behavioural-level reinforcement learning, replicating extinction, renewal, and reacquisition.
mapping.ree_translation: Direct biological grounding for R3 option (i) — score-aggregation level gating at the BG / cortico-striatal interface. The D1 / D2 dual-pathway architecture is the analog of REE's E3 score-aggregation site: action selection (D1 analog) vs action suppression (D2 analog) co-operating during learning and competing during extinction maps cleanly to E3's positive vs negative score components. ARC-062's weak-reading discriminator could route to this site rather than to PFC top-down score_bias: the gated-policy heads produce contributions that feed the cortico-striatal plasticity rule, not the PFC bias-projection. This is the architectural alternative to R3 option (iii) that the framework most directly licenses.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper is about *reinforcement-driven* action selection, not rule-context discrimination. The bridge from 'dopamine-gated cortico-striatal plasticity' to 'rule-context-conditioned policy gating' requires a separate commitment about what the rule signal IS at the BG synapse. Three reasonable options: (a) the rule signal modulates the dopamine-PE itself, (b) the rule signal modulates the cortical input to the synapse, (c) the rule signal modulates D1/D2 balance directly. The paper does not arbitrate among these.
mapping.source_context: Computational modelling integrating in vitro cortico-striatal plasticity data with behavioural reinforcement-learning theory; validated against operant extinction, renewal, and reacquisition data.

confidence_components.notes: Strong source quality — PLoS Biology, bridges-levels integration, replicates multiple behavioural phenomena. Mapping fidelity reduced because the framework targets reinforcement-driven action selection rather than rule-context gating; the architectural-site reading is a structurally analogous but not directly-tested extension. Transfer risk moderate: REE's E3 score-aggregation has direct analogues to D1/D2 cooperative-then-competitive signatures, but the dopamine-PE gating mechanism does not have a clean V3 substrate yet.
